name: Cyber Deconfliction
shortDescription: Coordinating cyber activity between authorised teams so defenders can tell sanctioned operations apart from real attacks — and so offensive teams don't trip over each other.
longDescription: Cyber deconfliction is the process of sharing — usually in advance — the *who, what, where, and when* of planned cyber activity so that other parties touching the same systems don't misinterpret it or interfere with it. Without deconfliction, a red-team engagement can trigger a full incident-response activation; two pen-test crews can scan each other and confuse evidence; a threat hunter can quietly burn a long-running surveillance operation by alerting the target. In practice, deconfliction takes the form of a shared channel (often a dedicated Slack/Teams room, ticket, or coordination cell) where the offensive side posts source IPs, target scope, time windows, and indicators they expect to generate, and the defensive side acknowledges so their SOC can suppress, tag, or quietly observe rather than escalate. The same mechanism is used between government agencies, between an MSSP and its customer, and between sister business units sharing infrastructure.
example: A purple-team exercise is scheduled to run from 02:00–06:00 UTC against the corporate VPN. Before kickoff, the red team posts to the #cyber-deconfliction channel: "Source: 198.51.100.42, target: vpn.corp.example.com, technique: password spraying against M365, expected IOCs: 4625 spikes from that IP, signed engagement letter ENG-2026-014." The SOC lead acknowledges and tags any matching alerts as `EXERCISE` so on-call analysts don't page the IR team at 03:00 and don't burn time pivoting on what looks like a real brute-force attack. After the window closes, the red team posts "complete" and the SOC re-arms the suppression rules. If, during the window, a *different* alert fires that doesn't match the deconfliction post, the SOC treats it as real — deconfliction reduces noise without disabling detection.
